Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) old = 'In this topic we cover how Agile Teams can impact the product vision and roadmaps. Lets get into the content' new = 'We just spent time on the customer \\u2014 understanding who they are, what they feel, what they need. Now the question becomes: how does that understanding connect to what your team actually builds?

That bridge is the product vision and roadmap.

A lot of teams think vision and roadmap are someone else\\u2019s job \\u2014 Product Management sets the direction, and the team just executes. And while it\\u2019s true that Product Management owns the vision, Agile Teams are not passive receivers of it. You shape it. Every demo, every piece of customer feedback, every impediment you surface \\u2014 that\\u2019s you influencing the roadmap.

In this section we\\u2019re going to look at how that works in practice: how vision flows down to your team, how your work flows back up to inform strategy, and how to make sure what you\\u2019re building today is still connected to where the organization is trying to go tomorrow.

' count = content.count\(old\) print\('Found', count\) content = content.replace\(old, new\) with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done.'\) ")

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) old = 'Product vision describes the future state of the product and aligns people on the direction of the product. It helps ensure everyone is working toward the same result and understands the customer environment and market needs.\\n \\n
' new = 'Think about the last time you were working on a feature and someone asked \\u201cwhy are we building this?\\u201d If the team hesitated \\u2014 that\\u2019s a vision problem.

Product vision describes the future state of the product. Not what it does today, but what it\\u2019s becoming and why that matters. It answers the question: what problem are we ultimately solving, and for whom?

A strong vision does two things: it aligns people on direction, and it gives teams the context to make good decisions without needing approval on every detail. When your team understands the vision, they can look at two options and instinctively know which one moves the product in the right direction.

Without it, you get teams that build features that individually make sense but collectively take the product nowhere in particular.

\\n \\n

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) old = 'The ART backlog includes feature and enabler work for a single ART.\\n \\n
' new = 'Coming out of vision, the question becomes: how does all that strategic intent actually get organized into work?

That\\u2019s where the ART Backlog comes in.

The ART Backlog is the holding area for all the work that needs to happen at the ART level \\u2014 specifically Features and Enablers. Features are the capabilities that deliver value to the customer. Enablers are the technical or infrastructure work that supports future delivery \\u2014 things like architecture, exploration, or compliance work that doesn\\u2019t show up as customer-facing but has to happen.

The key thing to understand: the ART Backlog is not your team\\u2019s backlog. It sits one level up. Your team pulls from it \\u2014 but what goes into it is driven by the product vision and roadmap we just discussed. That\\u2019s the connection. Vision shapes the ART Backlog, and the ART Backlog shapes what your team works on.

\\n \\n

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"101\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) old = content[script_content_start:script_end] print\('OLD:', old[:80]\) new = 'Here\\u2019s an analogy that makes this click for most teams: imagine you\\u2019re building a city. You can\\u2019t just build houses \\u2014 you need roads, water pipes, electricity grids in place first. Without that infrastructure, every new building takes ten times longer and costs twice as much.

The Architectural Runway is that infrastructure for your software.

It\\u2019s the existing code, components, and technical foundation that lets your team implement new features quickly \\u2014 without having to stop and redesign everything from scratch each time. When the runway is healthy, features flow. When it\\u2019s not, every feature becomes a struggle.

Here\\u2019s the catch: building features consumes the runway. Every time you deliver something new, you\\u2019re using up some of that foundation. So teams have to continuously invest in Enablers \\u2014 the work that extends the runway \\u2014 alongside the feature work. If you only ever build features, eventually the runway runs out and your velocity collapses.

This is why in SAFe, Enablers are not optional nice-to-haves. They\\u2019re how you protect your ability to keep delivering.

' content = content[:script_content_start] + new + content[script_end:] with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done.'\) ")

script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\)

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"103\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) new = 'We\\u2019ve been talking at the ART level \\u2014 vision, roadmaps, backlog. Now let\\u2019s zoom in to where your team lives every day: Epics, Features, and Stories.

These three levels are how large, strategic intentions get broken down into work a team can actually pick up and deliver in a sprint.

Think of it as a hierarchy. An Epic is a big business initiative \\u2014 too large to deliver in one PI, sometimes spanning multiple ARTs. A Feature is a slice of that epic that a single ART can deliver in a PI. A Story is what your team delivers in a single iteration.

Each level answers a different question. Epics answer: what big bets are we making? Features answer: what capabilities are we building this PI? Stories answer: what are we completing this week?

By the end of this section you\\u2019ll know how to write stories that are clear, testable, and properly sized \\u2014 and why that matters more than most teams realize.

' content = content[:script_content_start] + new + content[script_end:] with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done.'\) ")

chunk = content[idx:idx+200000]
note_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"note\\"'\)
script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\)
print\('NOTE:', chunk[note_start+13:note_start+600]\)

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"104\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) new = 'A feature isn\\u2019t just a label on a sticky note. A well-defined feature has three parts, and all three matter.

First, the name \\u2014 a short phrase that gives context. Not \\u201cimprove performance\\u201d but \\u201creduce checkout load time to under 2 seconds for mobile users.\\u201d Specific enough that anyone reading it knows what it means.

Second, the benefit hypothesis \\u2014 the why. What measurable benefit does this deliver to the user or the business? This is important because it\\u2019s how you know if the feature actually worked. If you can\\u2019t state the expected benefit before you build it, how will you know if you succeeded?

Third, acceptance criteria \\u2014 the conditions that must be true for the feature to be considered done. This includes both functional requirements \\u2014 what the system does \\u2014 and non-functional requirements like performance, security, or accessibility.

Here\\u2019s why this matters in practice: when a feature is poorly defined, every team member fills in the gaps with their own assumptions. You end up with five people building five different things, all technically correct, but none of them what the customer actually needed.

A well-defined feature eliminates that ambiguity before it becomes a problem.

' content = content[:script_content_start] + new + content[script_end:] with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done.'\) ")

script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\)
print\('NOTE:', chunk[note_start+13:note_start+600]\)

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"106\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) new = 'One of the most practical skills an Agile team can develop is knowing how to split stories well. A story that\\u2019s too big can\\u2019t fit in an iteration. But split it the wrong way and you end up with pieces that deliver no value on their own.

Two widely used frameworks for splitting \\u2014 from Richard Lawrence, Peter Green, and Dean Leffingwell \\u2014 give you structured ways to identify how a feature breaks down into stories that are small, independent, and deliverable.

Here\\u2019s an important distinction though: these techniques work well for splitting stories, but they don\\u2019t translate directly to splitting features. Features have different rules. For example, you should never defer the non-functional requirements of a feature \\u2014 like performance or security \\u2014 into a later feature. If you do, you\\u2019re shipping something incomplete and potentially unacceptable, even if all the functional stories are done.

Think of it this way: a story can be a thin vertical slice of functionality. But a feature needs to be a complete, valuable capability \\u2014 NFRs included. The split happens at the story level, not by cutting corners on the feature.

' content = content[:script_content_start] + new + content[script_end:] with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done.'\) ")

script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\)
print\('NOTE:', chunk[note_start+13:note_start+600]\)

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"107\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) new = 'Not every story in your backlog delivers something a user can see or click. Some of the most important work a team does is invisible to the customer \\u2014 and that\\u2019s exactly what Enabler Stories are for.

Enabler stories support the technical foundation: infrastructure, architecture, exploration. They\\u2019re written in technical language rather than user-centric language, because their audience is the engineering team, not the end user.

Here\\u2019s an example. An infrastructure enabler might read: \\u201cAs an engineer, I want to integrate CircleCI with Docker so we can build and test our microservices in an environment that mirrors our production setup.\\u201d No customer asked for that. But without it, every deployment is slower and riskier.

An architecture enabler might be about adding a component to handle authentication centrally \\u2014 again, invisible to users, but it enables five future stories to be built faster and more securely.

The key point: Enabler Stories are not technical debt cleanup disguised as stories. They\\u2019re deliberate investments in your runway. If your team never has any enablers in the backlog, that\\u2019s a warning sign \\u2014 it usually means technical debt is quietly accumulating until it forces a crisis.

' content = content[:script_content_start] + new + content[script_end:] with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: f.write\(content\) print\('Done p.107.'\) ")

idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"108\\"'\)
chunk = content[idx:idx+200000]

script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\)
print\('NOTE:', chunk[note_start+13:note_start+600]\)
print\('---'\)
print\('SCRIPT:', chunk[script_start+14:script_start+400]\)

Bash(/c/Windows/py -c " with open\('I:/Mon disque/Canada 2026/02 - SAFe Training/AI-Empowered SAFe for Teams \(Trainer Enablement 6.0\)/delivery-script.html', 'r', encoding='utf-8'\) as f: content = f.read\(\) idx = content.find\('data-page=\\"108\\"'\) chunk = content[idx:idx+200000] script_start = chunk.find\('class=\\"script\\"'\) full_script_start = idx + script_start script_content_start = full_script_start + len\('class=\\"script\\" contenteditable=\\"true\\">'\) script_end = content.find\('', script_content_start\) new = 'There\\u2019s a simple model that helps teams write better stories every time \\u2014 it\\u2019s called INVEST, developed by Bill Wake. Let\\u2019s walk through it.

Independent \\u2014 stories should be self-contained so they can be delivered in any order without depending on another unfinished story. Dependencies create drag.

Negotiable \\u2014 a story is not a contract. It\\u2019s a starting point for a conversation between the team and the Product Owner. The details get worked out together.

Valuable \\u2014 every story should deliver a vertical slice of value to the customer. If a story only makes sense to a developer and means nothing to a user, ask yourself whether it should be an enabler instead.

Estimable \\u2014 the team should be able to size it. If they can\\u2019t, it\\u2019s either too vague or too large.

Small \\u2014 small enough to complete within a single iteration. If it\\u2019s not, split it.

Testable \\u2014 you should be able to write a test for it before you build it. If you can\\u2019t define what \\u201cdone\\u201d looks like, you\\u2019re not ready to start.

One word of caution: don\\u2019t let INVEST become a mechanical checklist that distracts from the customer. Sizing stories correctly matters \\u2014 but the goal is always to deliver outcomes that matter to real people.
